QF.id: Income, Residential Status, Five Heads of Income_13

question.text: Mrs. Priya owns three houses — House A in Delhi (self-occupied), House B in Pune (self-occupied), and House C in Jaipur (vacant throughout the year). Under the income tax provisions, how will House C be treated?

answer.text: Self-occupied, since it is not let out
Deemed let-out, as only 2 houses can be claimed as self-occupied
Its annual value will be Nil since it is vacant
It will not be taxed at all since she does not earn any rent from it

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A taxpayer can select a maximum of 2 houses as self-occupied (annual value = Nil). Any additional house — even if kept vacant — is treated as "deemed let-out" and its notional annual value is computed and taxed under the head "Income from House Property." Mrs. Priya must choose 2 of her 3 houses as self-occupied; the third will be deemed let-out. **The Trap:** Candidates often think that a vacant property has Nil annual value. The law deems it let-out if the taxpayer already has 2 self-occupied houses, and notional rent becomes taxable income. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-A covers basic house property concepts. X-B tests the deemed let-out provision and multi-property scenarios. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Advise clients with multiple properties to strategically choose which 2 houses to declare self-occupied — pick the ones with the highest expected rent to save tax by keeping their annual value at Nil.
